top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: Saturday closes with three big stories pulling in different directions: Israel killed Hamas' top military commander in Gaza despite the fragile ceasefire, Trump left Beijing without real breakthroughs on Iran or Taiwan, and Wall Street ended the week firmly red on inflation jitters. Energy is the through-line — the Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut, oil is sitting above $109, and Cuba is now in a full diesel collapse with 22-hour blackouts. Markets and ceasefires both look brittle going into next week.

stories.headline: Israel kills Hamas' top military commander al-Haddad in Gaza City strike despite ceasefire
stories.summary: Israeli forces struck Gaza City on Friday evening, killing Izz al-Din al-Haddad, the head of Hamas' military wing and one of the last living architects of the October 7, 2023 attacks. Hamas confirmed his death and called it a 'treacherous assassination'. The strike killed at least seven and injured more than 50 — the heaviest blow to the fragile US-brokered Gaza ceasefire so far.

stories.headline: Trump leaves Beijing summit with handshakes but no breakthrough on Iran, Taiwan or trade
stories.summary: President Trump wrapped a two-day summit with Xi Jinping with a tentative 200-jet Boeing order and a vague 'strategic stability' framework, but no firm deals on the Strait of Hormuz, Taiwan arms or tariffs. Markets sold off because investors had priced in more. Trump kept the pending Taiwan arms package as a 'negotiating chip', while Xi warned that mishandling Taiwan would put the relationship in 'great jeopardy'.

stories.headline: Oil ends week above $109 as Strait of Hormuz stays effectively shut
stories.summary: Brent crude closed Friday at $107.04 and ran above $109 intraday, ending up 8.1% on the week as tanker traffic through Hormuz stayed at roughly 5% of normal. The IEA warned the market could remain severely undersupplied until October even if the shooting stops next month. India has already hiked petrol and diesel prices.

stories.headline: Wall Street sinks on inflation jitters; Nasdaq drops 1.5%, Dow off 537 points
stories.summary: The S&P 500 fell 1.24% to 7,408.50, the Nasdaq slid 1.54% to 26,225.14 and the Dow dropped 1.07% to 49,526.17 on Friday. Investors took profit on chip stocks after recent highs — Intel fell more than 6%, Micron 6.6%, AMD 5.7%, Nvidia 4.4% — as Treasury yields and oil weighed on sentiment.

stories.headline: Cuba runs out of diesel; Havana faces blackouts of up to 22 hours, protests break out
stories.summary: Cuba's energy minister confirmed on Thursday the island has run out of diesel and fuel oil after only one Russian tanker reached the country since January. Parts of Havana saw outages over 19 hours a day; eastern Cuba went fully dark. Small protests broke out in neighbourhoods west of the capital — a rare sight under the communist government.

stories.headline: Russia fires more than 200 drones at Ukraine overnight as three-day truce collapses
stories.summary: Zelenskyy said Russia launched over 200 drones at Ukrainian cities hours after a US-brokered three-day truce expired. Russia hit a record 8,000+ drones launched last month — the most since the 2022 invasion began. A prisoner swap of 1,000 each side did go through during the truce window.

stories.headline: Eurovision 2026 Grand Final goes live from Vienna under tight security
stories.summary: The Eurovision Song Contest Grand Final airs tonight from Wiener Stadthalle in Vienna, with 25 acts performing in front of a TV audience expected to top 160 million. Police have ringed the venue after weeks of protests over Israel's participation. Pre-contest press polls put Australia narrowly ahead of Sweden and France.

stories.headline: Hantavirus cruise ship Americans now isolating in Nebraska; 16 in federal quarantine unit
stories.summary: Eighteen US passengers from the MV Hondius arrived back in the US this week on a government repatriation flight to Offutt Air Force Base. Sixteen are at the National Quarantine Unit at Nebraska Medical Center; one mildly positive case is in biocontainment. WHO still pegs global public risk as low. Three deaths confirmed so far.

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: Today is a milestone day for Singapore's drug policy: first-time abusers who voluntarily surrender from May 16 are now placed on community supervision instead of being sent straight to a rehab centre. The weather is the usual May story — short thundery showers most afternoons, with a flash-flood warning in Jurong this morning. Dengue cases are down a striking 66% versus 2025, and police are appealing for help locating two missing teenage girls in separate cases.

stories.headline: From today: first-time drug abusers who surrender to CNB stay in the community instead of going to DRC
stories.summary: A landmark policy shift takes effect May 16: first-time drug abusers who voluntarily come forward to the Central Narcotics Bureau will no longer be automatically sent to the Drug Rehabilitation Centre. They go on community drug supervision with compulsory case management, counselling and regular urine or hair testing. Acting Minister Edwin Tong said the goal is to encourage more people to seek help early.

stories.headline: PUB flash-flood warning in Jurong West after morning downpour
stories.summary: PUB issued an alert urging motorists to avoid an area in Jurong West following a heavy morning deluge. Drains can usually cope with rainfall, but Saturday's burst was sharp enough to trigger flash flood risk on Upper Jurong Road. Inter-monsoon conditions mean short, thundery showers are expected on most afternoons through end-May.

stories.headline: Police appeal for help finding two missing teenage girls in separate cases
stories.summary: Singapore Police Force issued two appeals on Saturday seeking information on the whereabouts of two teenage girls in unrelated incidents. Anyone with information is asked to call 1800-255-0000 or submit it online via the SPF website. Public tip-offs remain the fastest way to close these cases.

stories.headline: Dengue cases down 66% year-on-year — over 600 cases so far in 2026
stories.summary: NEA said Singapore has logged just over 600 dengue cases between January 1 and May 15 — a 66% drop compared to the same period in 2025. The improvement reflects sustained mosquito-control efforts. MOH still warns that the inter-monsoon wet spell can rapidly rebuild breeding habitats; residents are urged to keep doing the 'Mozzie Wipeout' five-step routine.

stories.headline: Circle Line and TEL weekend service changes continue this evening
stories.summary: LTA reminds commuters that Circle Line trains end at 11:00pm tonight and only restart at 9:00am Sunday for final CCL6 integration testing. Shuttle buses 36 (HarbourFront–Bishan, every 7–10 min) and 37 (Bishan–Promenade, every 3–5 min) cover the gap. The Thomson-East Coast Line gets similar Friday-night and Saturday-morning adjustments starting next Friday, May 22, through July 4.

stories.headline: Weather outlook: short thundery showers most afternoons for the rest of May
stories.summary: The Meteorological Service Singapore says inter-monsoon conditions will prevail through end-May. Expect short, thundery afternoon showers on most days, with Sumatra squalls possible during the pre-dawn and morning hours on a few days — those bring widespread thunder and gusty winds. Daytime highs sit in the 33–34°C band, occasionally hitting 35°C.

stories.headline: Swatch x Audemars Piguet 'Royal Pop' launch draws queues at MBS, Ion and VivoCity
stories.summary: The much-hyped Swatch x Audemars Piguet 'Royal Pop' collaboration went on sale this morning at Marina Bay Sands, Ion Orchard and VivoCity. Carousell users were charging anywhere from S$40 to S$988 to queue on someone's behalf. Swatch says supplies are deliberately limited to manage demand.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: Three live theatres, all unsettled. The Ukraine ceasefire collapsed within hours of expiring and drones are flying again at record volume. The US-Israel war on Iran has the Strait of Hormuz still effectively shut, with Tehran saying it cannot trust Washington. And in Gaza, Israel just killed Hamas' top military commander al-Haddad despite a US-brokered ceasefire, while the Israel-Lebanon ceasefire got a 45-day extension. Nothing is exploding into all-out war today, but nothing is calming down either.

theatres.name: Russia–Ukraine War
theatres.status: Active — record drone war resumed after three-day truce expired

timeline.event: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine
War of attrition along eastern front; Russian gains slow but steady
Casualty estimate: ~1,000,000 Russian and 250,000–300,000 Ukrainian military killed/wounded since 2022
ISW data shows Russia loses net 45 sq miles for the first time in months
Putin says the war 'may be coming to an end', offers to meet Zelenskyy in a third country
US-brokered three-day truce holds; 1,000-for-1,000 prisoner exchange takes place
Truce expires; Zelenskyy reports more than 200 drones launched at Ukraine overnight
Ukraine strikes Russian gas facilities in Orenburg, 1,500km from the border, in retaliation
Russia logs 257 combat engagements in a single day; 8,000+ drones launched last month — a war record

theatres.name: US/Israel–Iran (Strait of Hormuz crisis)
theatres.status: Shaky ceasefire — strait still largely closed

timeline.event: US and Israel launch joint air campaign on Iran; Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei assassinated; Iran begins blocking the Strait of Hormuz
Hormuz traffic collapses from ~3,000 vessels/month to ~5% of normal; oil spikes
Day 50: Iran says it is closing Hormuz again
Ship seized off UAE and another sunk near Oman — oil surges further
US-Iran direct clash near Hormuz; war escalates
Trump calls Iran's response to US proposal 'totally unacceptable'
US starts effort to guide stranded ships through the strait; Chinese vessels allowed to sail Hormuz as Trump meets Xi; Netanyahu makes a secret UAE visit
Iran FM Araghchi says Tehran 'cannot trust the Americans at all' but is trying to keep the shaky ceasefire alive
Tensions flare again — a ship anchored off the UAE is seized and taken toward Iran; another is attacked

theatres.name: Israel–Lebanon / Gaza
theatres.status: Lebanon ceasefire extended 45 days; Gaza ceasefire shaken by killing of Hamas military chief

timeline.event: Gaza ceasefire takes effect after two years of war
First Israel–Lebanon ceasefire takes hold after Hezbollah losses
850+ killed in Gaza in Israeli strikes despite ceasefire being formally in place
Analysts warn Gaza risks becoming permanently divided; Hamas refuses to disarm
Israeli strikes on Lebanon rise sharply; commentators ask if the ceasefire is still real
Top UN official warns Gaza split risks becoming permanent
US-mediated talks produce a 45-day extension of the Israel–Lebanon ceasefire; next political round set for June 2–3, Pentagon security track May 29
Israel strikes Gaza City and kills Hamas military wing chief Izz al-Din al-Haddad along with at least six others; Hamas confirms his death
Hamas vows retaliation; mediators scramble to keep the Gaza ceasefire intact

hantavirus.label: Hantavirus Outbreak
hantavirus.ai_summary: The MV Hondius outbreak now stands at 11 cases (8 confirmed) and 3 deaths from Andes hantavirus, a Latin American strain. Eighteen Americans have been flown home — most are in Nebraska's federal quarantine unit, one is mildly positive and in biocontainment, and a symptomatic couple was sent on to Emory. The ship itself is due to dock in the Netherlands on May 18 for final disinfection. WHO still calls the public risk low, but the 42-day incubation window means more cases could surface in the next few weeks.

stories.headline: WHO: 11 Andes hantavirus cases, 3 deaths in cruise-ship cluster
stories.summary: As of WHO's May 13 update, the MV Hondius cluster stands at 8 confirmed and 3 suspected Andes hantavirus cases, with 3 deaths (two of which are virus-confirmed). The Andes strain is unusual for being capable of limited human-to-human transmission via close, prolonged contact. WHO has shipped 2,500 diagnostic kits from Argentina to labs in five countries.

stories.headline: 18 US passengers now in Nebraska after federal repatriation flight
stories.summary: American passengers disembarked the MV Hondius in the Canary Islands on Sunday and flew to Offutt Air Force Base on a US government medevac flight. Sixteen are isolating at the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center; one tested mildly positive and is in biocontainment. A symptomatic couple was sent on to Emory University's biocontainment facility in Atlanta.

stories.headline: MV Hondius due to dock in the Netherlands on May 18 for final disinfection
stories.summary: The Dutch-flagged cruise ship is currently at sea after disembarking passengers and is scheduled to make its final stop at a Netherlands port on May 18. Dutch and EU authorities will board for a deep-clean and rodent-control sweep before the ship is allowed to return to service.

stories.headline: ECDC keeps EU risk assessment at low but flags ongoing monitoring
stories.summary: ECDC's May 15 update keeps the EU/EEA risk rating low because human-to-human transmission of Andes virus requires very close contact. With passengers and crew from 23 countries (nine of them EU/EEA), authorities continue daily monitoring and contact tracing. New cases are still possible within the 42-day incubation window.

stories.headline: Boston travel influencer documenting 42-day quarantine from inside Omaha unit
stories.summary: Jake Rosmarin, a Boston-based travel influencer, has chosen to ride out the full 42-day Andes incubation period inside the federal quarantine unit at Nebraska Medical Center rather than self-isolate. His daily video updates have become one of the most-watched human looks inside America's only federal hantavirus quarantine facility.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Patch enterprise edge first this cycle. CISA's KEV deadline for the critical Cisco SD-WAN auth-bypass (CVE-2026-20182, CVSS 10.0) lands tomorrow — federal agencies must remediate by May 17. Microsoft's May Patch Tuesday closed 120 flaws including a wormable Netlogon RCE, and a fresh Exchange zero-day (CVE-2026-42897) surfaced two days later under active exploitation. The story of the cycle, though, is Google's disclosure of the first known AI-developed zero-day — a 2FA bypass that was about to be used in a mass-exploitation event.

stories.headline: Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN auth-bypass actively exploited — federal KEV deadline tomorrow
stories.summary: Cisco warned that an authentication bypass in Catalyst SD-WAN Controller is being exploited in zero-day attacks to gain administrative privileges. CISA added the flaw to the Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog and ordered federal civilian agencies to remediate by May 17. Cisco attributes the activity to UAT-8616, the same cluster deploying JSP-based 'XenShell' web shells on compromised devices.

stories.headline: Google detects first known AI-developed zero-day — a 2FA bypass primed for mass exploitation
stories.summary: Google said it identified an unknown threat actor using a zero-day exploit that was likely generated by an AI system — the first time AI has been seen used in the wild to discover a vulnerability and build a working exploit. The flaw is a 2FA bypass in a popular open-source sysadmin tool. Google says it intervened before the actor could launch a planned mass-exploitation event.

stories.headline: Critical wormable Windows Netlogon RCE in May Patch Tuesday
stories.summary: A stack-based buffer overflow in Windows Netlogon allows unauthenticated remote code execution against domain controllers — and Microsoft has flagged it as wormable. No zero-day exploitation yet, but a wormable DC bug is precisely the kind of flaw that becomes the next NotPetya if patches are delayed. Patch Tuesday addressed 120 flaws total with 17 rated Critical.

stories.headline: Microsoft confirms Exchange Server zero-day under active exploitation
stories.summary: Just two days after the quiet May Patch Tuesday, Microsoft confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of a new on-premises Exchange Server flaw. The bug is a spoofing vulnerability stemming from a cross-site scripting flaw. Admins should monitor Microsoft's advisories closely and apply the out-of-band fix as soon as it ships; Exchange remains one of the most-targeted enterprise services.

stories.headline: Windows zero-days 'YellowKey' and 'GreenPlasma' disclosed — BitLocker bypass and CTFMON privilege escalation
stories.summary: An anonymous researcher disclosed two more Windows zero-days codenamed YellowKey (a BitLocker bypass that gives access to protected drives) and GreenPlasma (a privilege escalation via the Windows Collaborative Translation Framework, CTFMON). Microsoft has patched the BitLocker side; ensure every endpoint is on the latest cumulative update, because full-disk encryption is no help if the OS can be tricked into unlocking it.

stories.headline: FortiClient EMS critical flaw actively exploited; CISA mandates fix
stories.summary: An improper-access-control bug in Fortinet's FortiClient EMS (versions 7.4.5 and 7.4.6) lets unauthenticated attackers execute code via crafted requests. WatchTowr says exploitation against its honeypots started on March 31. CISA added it to KEV and required federal patching by April 9. Apply the 7.4.7 hotfix immediately if not already done.

stories.headline: ShinyHunters claim 275M-record breach of Canvas LMS — largest ever in education
stories.summary: The ShinyHunters ransomware group says it has stolen roughly 275 million records — students, teachers, staff — from Canvas, the learning management system run by Instructure. Canvas was hit again on May 7, with its login page briefly replaced by a ransom note. Reports say 8,809 universities, schools and ministries are affected, making it the largest ed-tech breach on record.

stories.headline: West Pharmaceutical Services hit by ransomware; systems encrypted, data stolen
stories.summary: Drug-delivery component maker West Pharmaceutical Services confirmed a May 4 cyberattack that both encrypted internal systems and exfiltrated data. Healthcare supply chain players have become a consistent ransomware target through 2025–26; expect more affiliates to follow this playbook against suppliers rather than hospitals directly.
